title: Cat Overgrown Nails Clicking on Floor: Causes and Care Guide
meta_desc: Hearing clicking when your cat walks? Learn why overgrown nails happen, if it's an emergency, and how to help your feline friend safely.
content_html:
What is the clicking sound?

If you hear a distinct clicking or tapping sound when your cat walks on hard surfaces, it is usually a sign that their claws have grown too long. Unlike dogs, cat claws are normally retracted when walking. When they become overgrown, they extend past the paw sheath even when the cat is relaxed, causing them to strike the floor with every step. This is particularly common in senior cats who may have thicker nails and lower activity levels.

Is this an emergency?

The urgency level for clicking nails is typically Low. While it is not an immediate life-threatening situation, it is an issue that should be addressed promptly. If left untrimmed, the nail can continue to grow in a circle, eventually piercing the sensitive paw pad. If you notice your cat limping, licking their paw excessively, or if you see blood or discharge, the urgency increases as these are signs of an embedded nail or infection.

Why a photo helps with triage

Providing a clear, close-up photo of your cat’s paw is incredibly helpful for triage. It allows a professional to see the exact length of the claw and, more importantly, whether the tip of the nail has begun to curl back into the paw pad. A photo can help determine if this is a simple grooming task you can handle at home or if your cat requires a veterinary visit for a safe extraction and potential antibiotics.

faqs.question: How often should I trim my cat's nails?
faqs.answer: Most cats require a nail trim every 2 to 4 weeks. Senior cats or those who do not use scratching posts frequently may need more regular attention.

faqs.question: Can I trim an overgrown nail myself if it is touching the paw pad?
faqs.answer: If the nail is just touching the pad, you may be able to trim it carefully. However, if the nail has actually pierced the skin of the paw pad, do not attempt to pull it out at home; seek veterinary care to avoid causing more pain or worsening an infection.

faqs.question: Why do my senior cat's nails grow so fast and thick?
faqs.answer: As cats age, they often become less active and spend less time grooming and scratching, which naturally sheds the outer layers of the nail. This leads to thicker, faster-growing claws that require more frequent manual trimming.
